check: 51 states present in all tables
question: Do we have all 51 states in every table?

check: No duplicate keys
question: Is (state, series, month) a unique key?

check: No nulls in key fields
question: Are key fields complete (no nulls)?

check: All rates in a plausible bounded range (0-40%)
question: Are all rates in a plausible bounded range?
note: a rate is bounded - a value near 0 or above ~30 (COVID peak) is the natural check, unlike an unbounded count

check: Rates quantized to 0.1pp as published
question: Are rates quantized to 0.1pp as published?
note: the 0.1pp granularity means sub-0.05pp revisions round to zero (relevant in V4)

check: All obs_date are month-starts (monthly reference convention)
question: Are all observation dates month-starts?

check: Missing months: systematic (shared) vs idiosyncratic (per-state)
question: Are there missing months in any state?
threshold: 0 idiosyncratic gaps; shared BLS-wide holes documented
note: Oct-2025 is absent for every state AND for national UNRATE - a BLS data-availability event (the late-2025 federal shutdown delayed the release). Not a fetch error; the PIT availability frontier skips it automatically. RULE: do not interpolate the rate across this hole.

check: Panel edges aligned across states
question: Do all states cover the same window?
note: balanced monthly panel 1976-01 onward

check: ALFRED vintage boundary identified
question: How far back can point-in-time be reconstructed?
note: pre-boundary history exists only as current (revised) values: usable for climatology, NOT for point-in-time backtesting

check: National UNRATE vintages land on the jobs-report day (Friday)
question: Does the vintage calendar match the jobs-report schedule?
note: the national Employment Situation is released the first Friday after the reference month

check: State publication lag ~50 days (joint state emp/unemp report)
question: Is the state publication lag ~50 days?
note: released together with state nonfarm payrolls, ~3 weeks after the national jobs report

check: State UR published after the national jobs report
question: Do state releases follow the national jobs report?
metric: 100.0% of state months released after national; median gap +14d
note: state detail follows the national aggregate; a state rate is never available before the national UNRATE for the same month

check: Publication-lag regime stable over time
question: Is the publication-lag regime stable over time?
note: a stable lag lets a backtest assume a single availability rule

check: Release dates weakly increasing in obs order (per state)
question: Do months become available in order?
note: the PIT builder uses the cummax release frontier, so small inversions are harmless

check: PIT panel look-ahead guard (ur_latest_obs released <= as_of_date)
question: Does the PIT panel contain any look-ahead?

check: Endpoint revision rate & magnitude (first release vs current, pp)
question: How often/how much does a first print get revised?
metric: 86% of months revised; exact-zero 14%, within +-0.1pp 25%, within +-0.5pp 84%, max |4.1|pp
note: between claims (~0%) and NFP (~99%): most revisions are within the 0.1pp rounding, but benchmark years and COVID produce >1pp moves - so backtests still need vintage data

check: Larger revisions cluster at the annual benchmark (winter release months)
question: Do larger revisions cluster at the benchmark?
metric: 99% of largest revisions land in Jan-Apr; modal release month = 3 (1581 of 1656 obs across CA/TX/NY)
threshold: >= 50% of biggest revisions in the winter benchmark window
note: the annual LAUS re-estimation/re-seasonal-adjustment lands each winter; apply it on the release date, not spread backward

check: Systematic bias in first prints
question: Is there a systematic bias in first prints?
metric: mean revision -0.047pp (first prints overstate on average)
note: a small persistent sign is expected from benchmark direction; large would mean first prints are predictably off

check: National UNRATE revision benchmark quantified
question: How large is the national revision (calibration)?
note: the national rate is revised on the same schedule; its magnitude anchors state expectations

check: Extreme monthly moves classified (|MoM change| > 1.5pp)
question: Which extreme moves are real (COVID) vs unexplained?
note: the 2020 spike/rebound dominates; the handful of non-COVID extremes are genuine early-1980s and 2008-09 recession months - regime-flag rather than winsorize away

check: No implausibly long flat stretches (>= 12 identical monthly rates)
question: Are there stale/frozen stretches?
threshold: < 5 (short flats are normal for a quantized rate)
note: short repeats are expected at 0.1pp granularity; a 12+ month flat run flags a very low-volatility state or a data hold - inspect the listed cases, do not auto-clean

check: COVID unemployment spike present and correctly sized
question: Is the COVID spike present and correctly sized?
note: a sanity check that the largest real event is present and not truncated/mis-scaled

check: Labor-force-weighted mean of state rates tracks national UNRATE
question: Does the LF-weighted mean track national UNRATE?
threshold: weighted error < 0.2pp (and clearly better than unweighted)
note: rates cannot be summed; small high-unemployment states bias the naive mean, so weighting by labor force is required to reproduce the national aggregate

check: Cross-state co-movement (mean pairwise corr of YoY pp change)
question: Does every state co-move with the national cycle?
note: unemployment cycles are highly synchronised; a decorrelated state is a data suspect

check: Seasonally-adjusted rate free of residual seasonality
question: Did SA leave the rate free of residual seasonality?
metric: month-of-year R2 of MoM pp change (SA): 0.004
note: strong residual seasonality would mean the published SA is inadequate at the monthly frequency

check: Rate is highly persistent but bounded (cyclical, not trending)
question: Is the rate persistent but bounded (not trending)?
metric: California: AR1(level) 0.98, ADF p 0.02; Texas: AR1(level) 0.97, ADF p 0.02; New York: AR1(level) 0.96, ADF p 0.01; Florida: AR1(level) 0.98, ADF p 0.02; Ohio: AR1(level) 0.97, ADF p 0.08
note: unlike trending NFP, the rate mean-reverts over the cycle - persistent month-to-month but bounded; model the level with strong AR terms or the pp change

check: Monthly pp changes stationary with mild momentum
question: Are monthly pp changes stationary?
metric: all sample changes ADF-stationary: True; median AR1(change) 0.75
threshold: pp changes stationary (p<0.05) for all sample states
note: the pp change is the safe stationary transform; mild positive momentum supports change features

check: PIT features indexed by information time (monthly as-of grid)
question: Are features indexed by information time?
metric: monthly as-of grid: True; median data-staleness 2 months
note: at as-of month T the latest available reference month is ~T-2

check: Orientation: unemployment rate moves opposite to payroll growth (Okun sign)
question: Does the rate move opposite to payrolls (Okun)?
metric: California: corr -0.94; Texas: corr -0.90; New York: corr -0.94; Florida: corr -0.90; Ohio: corr -0.90
note: validates sign/alignment wiring: rising unemployment <-> falling payroll growth. NOT the analysis

check: Monthly alignment convention documented
question: How to align with monthly indicators?
metric: UR references the week incl. the 12th; released with state NFP
note: UR and state NFP share a reference period AND a release date, so they join directly with no relative timing offset - simpler than pairing across different reports

check: PIT coverage complete after ramp-in
question: From which date is the PIT panel complete?
note: usable backtest window starts 2008-08-01

check: Universe stability (balanced panel)
question: Is the universe stable?

check: Current-vs-vintage contamination if PIT were ignored
question: How badly would current-instead-of-vintage contaminate a backtest?
note: smaller than NFP but non-trivial: a backtest on current data would use benchmark-revised rates unknown in real time. Use vintage features (ur_pit_features.csv)

check: Manifest written (hashes, params, ranges)
question: Can the dataset be reproduced and audited?
note: ALFRED queries with pinned realtime params are reproducible; re-fetch and diff hashes to audit
